title: The Broken Keyboard Scanner
statement: Arjun's keyboard has a strange defect: whenever he types the character '#', it functions as a backspace, deleting the character immediately preceding it. If he types '#' when there is nothing to delete, it simply does nothing. Given a string S representing the sequence of keystrokes Arjun typed, return the final string that will appear on the screen.
input_format: A single string S consisting of lowercase English letters and the character #.
output_format: A single string representing the final text on the screen.

test_cases.explanation: The 'b' is deleted by the following '#'.
The 'd' is deleted, then 'c' is deleted. Final string is 'ab'.
The 'a' is deleted by first '#'. Second '#' does nothing. Then 'b' is typed.
Edge case: every character is immediately deleted.

title: Perfect Team Weight
statement: You are forming a team for a tug-of-war competition. You have an array W representing the weights of N candidates. According to competition rules, a "Perfect Team" consists of exactly two people whose combined weight is exactly equal to a target weight K. Determine if it is possible to form at least one Perfect Team from the candidates.
input_format:
The first line contains two integers: N (number of candidates) and K (target weight).
The second line contains N space-separated integers representing the array W.
output_format: Print YES if a Perfect Team can be formed, otherwise print NO.

test_cases.explanation: Edge case: Not enough candidates to form a team.

title: Mystic Sequences
statement: A mystic sequence is defined as an array of integers where the absolute difference between any two adjacent elements is strictly alternating between odd and even. That means if the first difference is odd, the second difference must be even, the third odd, and so on (or vice versa). Given an array `A` of size `N`, determine if it is a mystic sequence.
input_format:
The first line contains an integer N.
The second line contains N space-separated integers for the array A.
output_format: Print YES if the sequence is a mystic sequence, otherwise print NO.

test_cases.explanation: Differences are 3 (odd), 2 (even), 5 (odd). Pattern is odd, even, odd.
Differences are 2, 2, 2. The parity does not alternate.
Only one difference (5, odd). Valid mystic sequence.
Edge case: N=1 is inherently valid as there are no adjacent differences.

title: Balanced Brackets Reloaded
statement: You are given a string containing only the characters '(', ')', and '?'. The '?' character functions as a wildcard that can be replaced by either '(' or ')'. Determine if it is possible to configure the wildcards such that the entire string becomes a balanced bracket sequence.
output_format: Print YES if it can be balanced, otherwise print NO.
constraints: length of S will always be strictly even for valid inputs.

test_cases.explanation: Replacing '?' with ')' makes it (()), which is balanced.
No valid replacement: ')' at index 1 cannot be matched since there's no opening bracket before it.

title: The Golden Ratio Filter
statement: You are a cryptographer decrypting a stream of numbers. You only extract the valid "Golden Numbers". A number is considered valid if the sum of its digits is a multiple of its length (number of digits). Given an array of N integers, find how many of them are valid Golden Numbers.
input_format:
The first line contains an integer N.
The second line contains N integers separated by spaces.
output_format: A single integer denoting the count of Golden Numbers.

test_cases.explanation: 12: sum=3, len=2 (no). 333: sum=9, len=3 (yes). 45: sum=9, len=2 (no).
Edge case: All multiples of 10 have length 2 and sum 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. None are divisible by 2.
All are length 2 with sums 2, 4, 6 respectively, all multiples of 2.

title: Grid Navigator
statement: A robot starts at position (0,0) on a 2D Cartesian plane. It receives a string of commands consisting of 'U' (Up, y+1), 'D' (Down, y-1), 'L' (Left, x-1), and 'R' (Right, x+1). Determine if the robot returns to the exact starting coordinates (0,0) after executing all commands.
input_format: A single string containing the characters U, D, L, R.
output_format: Print YES if the robot returns to (0,0), otherwise print NO.

test_cases.explanation: Up, Down, Left, Right cancels out, ends at (0,0).

title: The Odd Server Out
statement: In a data center, all servers are paired together by assigning them the same unique ID, for redundancy. However, one server's pair failed and was removed, leaving it without a matching backup. Given an array of N integers representing the server IDs, find the ID of the single server that is missing a pair.
input_format:
The first line contains an odd integer N.
The second line contains N space-separated integers representing the server IDs.
output_format: Print the ID of the standalone server.

test_cases.explanation: Pairs are 4 and 2. 1 has no pair.
Edge case: only one server exists.

title: Consecutive Coin Runs
statement: You are given a string composed exclusively of 'H' (Heads) and 'T' (Tails). A "run" is an uninterrupted sequence of identical consecutive coin flips. Find the length of the longest run in the given sequence.
input_format: A single string containing only characters 'H' and 'T'.
output_format: An integer representing the length of the longest consecutive run.

test_cases.explanation: The longest run is HHHH with length 4.
The entire sequence is a single run.
Longest run TTTT is of length 4.

title: Temperature Anomaly
statement: You are analyzing temperature data over N days. A day is considered an "anomaly" if its temperature is strictly greater than both the previous day's and the next day's temperatures. Find the number of anomaly days. The first and last day can never be anomalies because they lack a previous or next day, respectively.
input_format:
The first line contains N (number of days).
The second line contains N integers representing the daily temperatures.
output_format: A single integer representing the total count of anomalies.

test_cases.explanation: Edge case: N <= 2 means peaks are impossible.

title: Anagram Verifier
statement: Given two strings A and B consisting of lowercase English letters, determine if B is an anagram of A. An anagram is formed by rearranging the letters of a string, using all original letters exactly once.
input_format: Two lines containing strings A and B respectively.
output_format: Print YES if they are anagrams, NO otherwise.

test_cases.explanation: Edge case: Different lengths means they cannot be anagrams.
